behaviors.description: The cron parser rejects invalid expressions (e.g., month 13, day 32, malformed syntax) with a descriptive error message.
behaviors.justification: SEAM-MAP: local function seam (string -> parse result/error). The failure is parser validation/message construction logic inside one component; no module handoff, OS, network, subprocess, or model output is required. REJECTION-GATE: Integration/System/Agentic/Workflow were rejected because behavior text is limited to parser rejection + message quality, with no external seam.
behaviors.test_description: Call the parser directly with invalid cron inputs (`13` month, `32` day, malformed tokens) and assert it returns failure plus field-specific descriptive error text.

behaviors.description: The next-run-time calculator returns correct timestamps for standard cron expressions, including edge cases like "last day of month" and "nearest weekday."
behaviors.justification: SEAM-MAP: local function/object seam (schedule object + start time -> next N timestamps). The risk is date-calculation algorithm correctness; deterministic inputs can expose real failures without infrastructure. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered but rejected because the behavior is specifically calculator correctness, not parser-to-calculator wiring.
behaviors.test_description: Construct schedule objects for representative cron patterns (including last-day and nearest-weekday cases), call next-run computation from fixed start times, and assert exact expected timestamp sequences.

behaviors.description: The overlap detector identifies when two schedules would fire within the configured minimum gap (e.g., 5 minutes) and reports the conflicting time windows.
behaviors.justification: SEAM-MAP: module/interface seam (schedule generation outputs handed to overlap detector with gap policy). Real failures often occur in boundary interpretation (window math, inclusive/exclusive edges, handoff of generated times), not just isolated arithmetic. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no real OS/network/subprocess dependency, (b) no AI/ML judgment, (c) no multi-agentic chain; so Integration is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Wire parser + next-run generator + overlap detector in-process: feed two cron expressions and a 5-minute gap, generate windows over a horizon, and assert detected conflict windows match expected boundaries.

behaviors.description: Timezone-aware scheduling produces correct run times across a DST transition — a job scheduled for 2:30 AM handles the spring-forward gap and fall-back ambiguity correctly.
behaviors.justification: SEAM-MAP: module/interface seam between scheduler logic and timezone/calendar library semantics. The failure mode is correct interpretation of DST gap/ambiguity when scheduling logic composes with TZ rules. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no required real subprocess/network/OS event boundary, (b) no AI/ML output dependency, (c) no multi-agentic orchestration. REJECTION-GATE: System was considered but rejected because behavior text requires DST correctness in scheduling semantics, which can be exercised in-process with real timezone rules.
behaviors.test_description: Run scheduler with a real timezone (e.g., one that has DST), fixed dates around spring-forward and fall-back, and a `2:30 AM` cron; assert expected handling for nonexistent and ambiguous local times.

behaviors.description: When a scheduled job's computed next run time has already passed (e.g., system was down), the engine determines whether to skip or backfill based on the job's configured missed-run policy.
behaviors.justification: SEAM-MAP: module/interface seam across next-run computation + policy evaluator + scheduler decisioning. The real failure boundary is policy application to computed schedule state across components (skip vs backfill), not a single pure function in isolation. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no mandatory real OS/network/subprocess dependency, (b) no AI/ML judgment, (c) no multi-agentic chain.
behaviors.test_description: In-process integration test with controllable clock: compute missed run scenarios, apply `skip` and `backfill` policies, and assert the engine enqueues zero vs expected catch-up runs with correct timestamps.
